content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 28, 1992

Michael D. Martello
City Attorney
City of Concord
1950 Parkside Drive, MS 08
Concord, CA  94519-2578

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-92-471

Dear Mr. Martello:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Vice Mayor Mark DeSaulnier regarding his
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     May Vice Mayor DeSaulnier participate in Concord City Council
and Redevelopment Agency decisions pertaining to the financing of
the Cinema Square project where the vice mayor owns and operates a
restaurant located across a six-lane street from the site of the
project?

_______________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800  ●  P.O. Box 807  ●  Sacramento, CA  95812  ●  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-471
Page 2


CONCLUSION

     Decisions pertaining to the financing of the Cinema Square
project are too interlinked with the project as a whole to be
considered separately. Consequently, if the project will have a
material financial effect on the economic interests of the vice
mayor, he may not participate in the decisions pertaining to the
financing of the project.

     However, to the extent that later decisions pertaining to the
Cinema Square project are merely implementation decisions which
will not affect the existence of the project as a whole, and will
not independently materially affect an economic interest of the
vice mayor, the vice mayor may participate in the decisions.

FACTS

     The Concord City Council and Redevelopment Agency is
currently considering the construction of the complex located in
Concord's downtown redevelopment project area. The Concord Cinema
Square project (the "project") includes a movie theater multiplex
and 40,000 square feet of commercial retail/office space. In
addition, the project will contain 28 apartment units. The
project is expected to attract 800,000 visitors yearly to the
downtown area.

     The project is a joint venture between the Concord
Redevelopment Agency and a private developer. The redevelopment
agency owns the property and will own and operate the parking
facilities in the project. The developer will develop the balance
of the project.

     The project itself will include four restaurants and an ice
cream store. In addition, within a four-square block area
surrounding the project, there currently exist approximately 54
restaurants. Vice Mayor Desaulnier owns and operates a restaurant
located across a six-lane street from the site of the project.
You stated that he holds the property on which his restaurant is
situated by a long-term lease at a fixed rent. There are seven
years remaining on the lease. The restaurant's annual gross sales
are approximately one million.

     Although the city has already entered into an agreement to go
forward with the project (prior to the time the vice mayor assumed
office), the city council and redevelopment agency will be
considering a variety of decisions concerning the project, such as
construction and financing plans, and other similar items.

     You stated that on October 6, 1992, the city will be
considering the financing plan for the project. According to the
Disposition and Development Agreement, the plan will include a
cost breakdown and cash flow projection and the various possible
sources of funding.

     The city will review the plan to determine if the
contemplated financing will be reasonably available and will be

Our File No. A-92-471
Page 3


sufficient to cover the costs of the project. The city will also
review the plan to make sure that it is consistent with the
requirements of the Disposition and Development Agreement. The
failure of the city to approve or disapprove the plan will result
in the plan becoming effective as if adopted.

ANALYSIS

1.  Economic Interests

     Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

     Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

          (b)  Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

          (c)  Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

                    Section 87103(a), (b) and (c).

     In your letter, you stated that Vice Mayor DeSaulnier owns a
restaurant in the jurisdiction. Clearly, the vice mayor's
business is an economic interest. In addition, any person or
business that has made any payment of $250 or more to Vice Mayor
DeSaulnier or his business in the past 12 months is a source of
income to the vice mayor for purposes of Section 87103.2

     Finally, you stated that the vice mayor holds the property on
which the restaurant is situated by way of a long-term lease.

_______________________
2  You have not asked about sources of income. We have enclosed
general information for your convenience.

Our File No. A-92-471
Page 4


Section 82033 provides that an "interest in real property" includes any leasehold, beneficial, or ownership interest in real property located in the jurisdiction owned directly, indirectly, or beneficially by the public official if the fair market value of the interest is $1,000 or more.3  Consequently, the vice mayor may not participate in any decision which will have a foreseeable4 material financial effect on any of these economic interests.

2.  Materiality

The standard for materiality differs depending on the type of economic interest involved and whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly affected by the decision.  Regulation 18702.1 provides definitions for when economic interests are directly involved in a decision.  (See, Regulation 18702.1(a)(3) and (b).)  According to your facts, the vice mayor's interests are only indirectly affected.5

A.  The Leasehold Interest

The vice mayor has a leasehold interest in real property across the project site.  You stated that the vice mayor holds a long-term lease at a fixed rent, with seven years remaining.  Since the property interest is a leasehold interest, the indirect effect of a decision is material if:

1.  The decision will change the legally allowable use of the leased property (and the lessee has a right to sublease the property);

2.  The decision will foreseeably cause the lessee to change the actual use of the property;

_______________________
3  Regulation 18729(b) provides that the value of a leasehold interest is the amount of rent owed during a 12-month period.  Thus, if the vice mayor's payments on the lease are $1,000 or more annually, he has a leasehold "interest in real property," as defined in the Act.

4  Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.  An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur.  Certainty is not required.  However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.  (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

5  Please note that Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(D) provides that the effect of some redevelopment decisions is deemed to be material.  However, we are assuming for purposes of this letter that the decisions set forth in Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(D) have already been made (prior to the vice mayor's election) with respect to the project.

Our File No. A-92-471
Page 5


     3.  The decision will change the actual use of property
within 300 feet of the leased property, which will affect the use
or enjoyment of the leased property;

     4.  The decision will increase or decrease the amount of rent
for the leased property by $250 or 5 percent, whichever is
greater, during any 12-month period; or,

     5.  The decision will result in a change in the termination
date of the lease.

     You stated that none of these effects would occur with
respect to the leasehold interest.  If this is the case, the vice
mayor's leasehold interest would not be a disqualifying financial
interest under the Act.

     B.  The Restaurant

     Whether the indirect effect of a decision on a business
entity, such as the vice mayor's restaurant, is a material depends
on the financial size of the business entity.  For example,
Regulation 18702.2(g) provides that for a relatively small business entity, the indirect effect of a decision is material where:

          (1)  The decision will result in an increase
     or decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year
     of $10,000 or more; or

          (2)  The decision will result in the business
     entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
     reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
     fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or more; or

          (3)  The decision will result in the increase
     or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
     of $10,000 or more.

     We cannot make the factual determination as to whether the
project will materially affect the vice mayor's business.  We must
leave this factual determination of materiality to you and the
vice mayor within the guidelines provided by Regulation 18702.2.
However, if the decisions will not affect the gross revenues of
the vice mayor's business by $10,000 or more for a fiscal year, or
the value of its assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more, or
affect the expenses of the vice mayor's business by $2,500 or
more, the vice mayor's business interest will not be considered a
disqualifying financial interest.

     3.  Governmental Decisions

     Generally, each governmental decision must be analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's economic interests.  (In re Owen
(1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.)  Thus, even if it is determined that the

Our File No. A-92-471
Page 6


vice mayor has a conflict of interest with respect to the project
as a whole, some future decisions may be merely implementation
decisions which may be separable from the decisions concerning the
project as a whole. (Wachob Advice Letter, No. I-91-464, copy
enclosed.)

You have asked specifically about an upcoming decision
pertaining to financing the project. In the Athan Advice Letter,
No. A-86-094 (copy enclosed), we advised:

[W]e conclude that Mayor Bennett must
disqualify himself from participating in decisions
of the San Ramon City Council/Redevelopment Agency
concerning the proposed Crow Canyon area. However,
we emphasize that this conclusion applies only to
the major policy decisions about the project, such
as project boundaries, financing decisions,
approval of the environmental impact report, types
of uses, and major public improvements in the
project area. Once the basic policy decisions have
been reached, Mayor Bennett may participate in the
decisions which implement, but do not change these
policies.

For example, if the City Council/Redevelopment
Agency votes to conduct an environmental impact
report (a basic decision from which Mayor Bennett
must disqualify himself), Mayor Bennett may
participate in the decision to choose the engineer
or consultant to whom the City will award the
contract to perform the EIR. We caution, however,
that most implementation decisions in which Mayor
Bennett may participate will not occur until some
time after the plans for the Crow Canyon Area
project have been approved. Furthermore...Mayor
Bennett must examine each decision to determine if
there are specific facts which require a contrary
conclusion.

Emphasis added.

In addition, in the Vickers Advice Letter, No. A-84-302 (copy
enclosed), we addressed the question of whether an official who
had an economic interest in an oil company which was involved in a
school board decision to relocate a school could participate in
other related decisions. We advised that the official could not
participate in the other decisions if "the matter under
consideration by the Board of Trustees is of such significance to
the implementation of either the school relocation plan or the
processing facility construction plan that it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision of the Board of Trustees could
result in termination of the agreement between the Board of
Trustees and the oil companies or a significant modification of
the agreement."

Our File No. A-92-471
Page 7


It would appear that the content of the financing plan, and
its approval or disapproval could result in the termination or a
significant modification of the project as a whole. This is true
despite the fact that the review of the plan will be subject to
criteria that is set forth in the Disposition and Development
Agreement. (See e.g., Sweeney Advice Letter, No. A-89-639; Roach
Advice Letter, I-91-405, copies enclosed.) For example, the
rejection of the plan could result in significant delays in the
project as a whole while a new financing plan is prepared.
Consequently, if the vice mayor has a conflict of interest
regarding the project as a whole, he may not participate in the
financing decisions regarding the project.

However, to the extent that later decisions pertaining to the
Cinema Square project are merely implementation decisions which
will not affect the existence of the project as a whole, and will
not independently materially affect an economic interest of the
vice mayor, the vice mayor may participate in the decisions. Of
course, even those decision that are considered to be
implementation decisions must still be analyzed to determine if
the specific decision will have a material financial effect on an
official's interests. To the extent that any of the
implementation decisions will independently materially affect a
financial interest, the officials may not participate. You have
not specified other decisions that may arise that may be merely
implementation decisions, thus we can only provide these general
guidelines. You should contact us for further advice as the
future decisions arise.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel


By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa
Enclosure

and Redevelopment Agency decisions pertaining to the financing of
the Cinema Square project where the vice mayor owns and operates a
restaurant located across a six—lane street from the site of the
proj ect?
, et seq.

Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Our File No. A-92-47l

CONCLUSION: Decisions pertaining to the financing of the Cinema Square
project are too interlinked with the project as a whole to be
considered separately.
Consequently, if the project will have a
material financial effect on the economic interests of the vice
mayor,
he may not participate in the decisions pertaining to the
financing of the project.
However, to the extent that later decisions pertaining to the
Cinema Square project are merely implementation decisions which
will not affect the existence of the project as a whole,
and will
not independently materially affect an economic interest of the
vice mayor, the vice mayor may participate in the decisions.

Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95812 ● (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-92-471 Page 2 CONCLUSION Decisions pertaining to the financing of the Cinema Square project are too interlinked with the project as a whole to be considered separately. Consequently, if the project will have a material financial effect on the economic interests of the vice mayor, he may not participate in the decisions pertaining to the financing of the project. However, to the extent that later decisions pertaining to the Cinema Square project are merely implementation decisions which will not affect the existence of the project as a whole, and will not independently materially affect an economic interest of the vice mayor, the vice mayor may participate in the decisions. FACTS The Concord City Council and Redevelopment Agency is currently considering the construction of the complex located in Concord's downtown redevelopment project area. The Concord Cinema Square project (the "project") includes a movie theater multiplex and 40,000 square feet of commercial retail/office space. In addition, the project will contain 28 apartment units. The project is expected to attract 800,000 visitors yearly to the downtown area. The project is a joint venture between the Concord Redevelopment Agency and a private developer. The redevelopment agency owns the property and will own and operate the parking facilities in the project. The developer will develop the balance of the project. The project itself will include four restaurants and an ice cream store. In addition, within a four-square block area surrounding the project, there currently exist approximately 54 restaurants.
